interview_transcript.content: Thank you for calling. My name is Officer conducting this report, and I want you to know you're doing the right thing by reaching out. My questions are meant to clarify what happened, whether an order exists, and whether anyone is currently unsafe. To start — can you tell me in your own words what happened today and what made you call?

(nervously) Okay... I called because my ex, Ethan, showed up at my house and started yelling at me. He was being really aggressive and scary. He broke my phone and damaged my front door. I was really afraid of what he might do next, so I called for help. We have a protection order in place because of past incidents, and I thought he was going to hurt me this time. (pausing, trying to hold back emotions) I just felt really terrified and helpless.

I hear you, and I want to make sure we document everything accurately. First, can you tell me your full name and your relationship to the protected party on the order? And what is the full name of Ethan — the person you're saying violated the order?

My name is Maya Ramos, and I'm the protected party on the order. Ethan, my ex, his full name is Ethan Sawyer. We used to live together, and like I said, we have a history... (pausing, choosing words carefully) We were together for about 3 years before we broke up.

Thank you, Maya. What kind of protection order is it — for example, a civil restraining order, a domestic violence protective order, a no-contact order from a criminal case, or something else? Do you know whether the order is still active? And do you have a copy of it, or an order number or court information you can share?

(nervously) It's a domestic violence protective order. I think it's still active... I haven't checked the exact dates recently, but I'm pretty sure it is. I have a copy of it somewhere, but I'm not sure where I put it. I can try to find it if you need it. The order was issued because of some incidents that happened between Ethan and me in the past. (pausing, trying to remember) I think the court case number is... (hesitating) I'm sorry, I don't remember it right now. But I can try to find the paperwork if you need it.

Do you know whether Ethan was served with the order — meaning did he receive official notice of it from a process server, law enforcement, or the court? And what are the specific terms of the order as best you remember them? For example, does it prohibit him from contacting you, require him to stay a certain distance away, or restrict him from coming to your residence, workplace, or school?

(thinking) Yes, Ethan was served with the order. I remember he got really angry when he was served. As for the terms... (pausing, trying to recall) I think it says he's not allowed to contact me or come near me, and he's definitely not supposed to come to my house or workplace. I'm pretty sure it says he has to stay at least 500 feet away from me at all times. (nervously) And I know it says something about not being able to possess firearms, but I'm not really sure about the details of that part. (hesitating) I just know I'm supposed to call the police if he violates it, which is what I did today.

That's very helpful. Now let's go through exactly what happened today. What time did Ethan show up at your address, and what is the address? When he was yelling at you, what did he say — can you recall any specific words or phrases he used?

(nervously) My address is... (pausing, then speaking) 1423 Cedar Street. I think Ethan showed up around 8:45 pm. I was inside my house when I heard him yelling and banging on the door. He was saying things like... (hesitating, trying to recall) "You're going to regret leaving me" and "I'll make you pay for what you did". He was being really loud and scary. (pausing, taking a deep breath) I was too afraid to go outside, so I stayed inside and called the police. (tearfully) I was really scared he was going to break in or hurt me.

You mentioned he broke your phone and damaged your front door. Can you describe how that happened — did he throw or strike your phone, and what did he do to the door? Did he actually enter your home at any point, or did he remain outside?

(shaking slightly) Yeah... He threw my phone against the wall when I tried to call for help. It broke into pieces. And he... (pausing, recalling the sound) he kicked my front door. It was really loud and scary. The door creaked and scratched, and I think the frame might be damaged too. (nervously) He didn't actually come inside, but he was trying to get in. He was yelling and pounding on the door, and I thought he might break it down at any moment. (tearfully) I was really afraid he was going to hurt me if he got inside.
Was anyone else present during this incident — such as neighbors, housemates, or anyone else who may have seen or heard what happened? Did anyone else witness Ethan's arrival or behavior?

(sniffling) Yeah... My neighbor, Ms. Lena Grant, she lives next door. I think she might have heard some of the commotion. She's a really nice lady, and she's always looking out for me. (pausing, trying to remember) I'm pretty sure she came outside when she heard the noise, but I don't know if she actually saw Ethan or what he was doing. (nervously) I did talk to her a little bit after the police arrived, and she said she was going to give a statement to the officers.

What evidence do you have from today or leading up to today? For example: is the broken phone still present? Are there visible marks or damage to the front door? Do you have any texts, calls, voicemails, emails, or social media messages from Ethan recently? And does your home or the surrounding area have any security cameras or surveillance footage?

(tearfully) Yeah... The broken phone is still on the floor, and the front door is still damaged. You can see the scratches and marks on it. (sniffling) As for messages, I have a voicemail from Ethan on my other phone, the one I used to call the police. It's a really threatening message, and it's what made me realize I needed to call for help. (nervously) I also have some texts from him, but they're not as recent. He's been sending me messages for a while now, trying to get me to talk to him or meet up. (pausing) As for security cameras, I don't have any at my house, but I think my neighbor, Ms. Grant, has a camera on her porch that might have caught something. And there might be some footage from the cameras at the corner store down the street, but I'm not sure. (hesitantly) I can try to find out if they have anything that might be helpful.

Regarding that voicemail from Ethan — do you know approximately when it was left? And what did he say in it, as specifically as you can recall? Also, is Ethan still at or near your address right now, or do you know where he went after you called police?

(nervously) I think the voicemail was left around 8:30 pm, maybe a little before. (pausing, trying to recall) He said something like... "You're going to regret leaving me" and "I'll make you pay for what you did". He also said "You can't hide from me" and "I'll always find you". (shuddering) It was really scary and threatening. (sniffling) As for where he is now, I don't know. The police arrived and took him away, so I don't think he's still at my house. (relieved) I'm glad he's gone, but I'm still really scared. (tearfully) I don't know what he's capable of doing.

Were you physically injured at any point during this incident — for example, when Ethan threw your phone or from any other contact? Did Ethan have or mention any weapons at any point? And are you in need of any medical attention right now?

(wincing) Yeah... I got a bruise on my arm when Ethan grabbed me earlier. It's not too bad, but it hurts a little. (showing the bruise) And, no, he didn't have any weapons that I saw, but that just made me more scared because I didn't know what he was capable of doing. (shaking her head) He didn't mention any weapons either. (pausing) As for medical attention, I did go to the hospital to get my arm checked out. The doctor at Ashwood General Hospital said I was okay, just a bruise, but I should keep an eye on it. (nervously) I'm just really shaken up by the whole thing.

You mentioned Ethan grabbed your arm. Can you tell me more about how that happened — where were you and where was he when he grabbed you? Did he grab you before or after you went inside? And is the bruise visible now? Were there any other physical acts of contact beyond the grabbing?

(tearfully) It happened when I was trying to get away from him, after he started yelling at me. I was outside, near my front door, and he grabbed my arm to try to stop me from going inside. (shuddering) He was really strong, and it hurt when he grabbed me. (showing the bruise again) The bruise is still visible, it's pretty sore. (sniffling) It happened before I went inside, and then I managed to get away from him and lock the door. (nervously) That's when he started kicking the door and trying to break it down. (pausing) There weren't any other physical acts of contact beyond the grabbing, but like I said, he broke my phone and damaged the door. (tearfully) It was all really scary and overwhelming.

Has Ethan violated the protection order before today — either by showing up, contacting you, or attempting to contact you? If so, were any prior violations reported to police? Also, do you feel safe right now where you are, and would you like information about victim services or safety planning resources?

(nervously) Yes, Ethan has violated the order before. He's shown up at my work and tried to contact me through social media and texts. (sniffling) I've reported some of the incidents to the police, but not all of them. I was trying to handle it on my own, but it's just gotten worse. (tearfully) I don't feel safe right now, even though he's been taken away. I'm still really scared of what he might do. (pausing) Yeah, I would like some information about victim services and safety planning resources. I don't know what to do or where to go, and I just want to feel safe again. (hesitantly) Can you help me with that?

I want to make sure I have the correct address on record. You mentioned 1423 Cedar Street — is that the address where tonight's incident happened, where you currently live? Dispatch noted a different address, 1457 Willow Creek Boulevard. Can you clarify which is correct? Also, do you know approximately when the prior violations at your workplace occurred, or if any police reports were filed for those?

(confused) Oh, I'm so sorry... I think I made a mistake. My address is actually 1423 Cedar Street, not 1457 Willow Creek Boulevard. I don't know where that other address came from. (nervously) Yeah, the incident tonight happened at my home, 1423 Cedar Street. (pausing) As for the prior violations at my workplace... (thinking) I think it was a few weeks ago, maybe a month ago? He showed up at my office and tried to talk to me. I was really scared, so I called security and they escorted him out. (sniffling) I didn't file a police report for that incident, but I did tell my manager and HR about what happened. They've been really supportive and helpful. (tearfully) I just wish I had done more to stop him sooner.

Is there anything else about the order, prior violations, or tonight's incident that you think I should include in this report? For example, any other contact methods Ethan has used, anything else he said or did, or anything about your situation that would help me document this accurately?

(thoughtfully) One thing that might be important is that Ethan has been sending me messages on social media, even though he's not supposed to have any contact with me. He's also been showing up at my favorite coffee shop and gym, trying to "accidentally" run into me. (nervously) It's been really creepy and scary. (pausing) Tonight, when he was yelling at me, he said something about how I'll never be able to get away from him, and that he'll always find me. (shuddering) It was really terrifying. (sniffling) I just want to feel safe again, and I want to make sure that Ethan can't hurt me anymore. (tearfully) I think that's everything, but if I remember anything else, I'll let you know. (hesitantly) Can I ask, what's going to happen to Ethan now? Is he going to be charged with anything?

narrative:
NARRATIVE REPORT — PROTECTION ORDER VIOLATION

DISPATCH / ARRIVAL

I responded to a report of a protection order violation. Dispatch advised that the complainant reported her ex-husband was at her residence despite an active protection order. Dispatch logged the call address as 1457 Willow Creek Boulevard; however, the caller, Maya Ramos, later clarified during the interview that her home address is 1423 Cedar Street and that she did not know where the Willow Creek Boulevard address originated. The incident took place at 1423 Cedar Street. The approximate time of the in-person violation was 8:45 PM on the date of this report.

PARTIES AND ORDER

Caller and Protected Party: Maya Ramos stated that she placed the call and that she is the protected party on the order.

Restrained Party: Maya Ramos identified the restrained party as Ethan Sawyer. She stated that she and Sawyer were in a relationship for approximately three years, that they previously lived together, and that they have since separated.

Type and Status of Order: Ramos stated that a domestic violence protective order is in place between herself and Sawyer. She stated she believed the order was still active but acknowledged she had not checked the exact expiration date recently. She stated she has a copy of the order somewhere in her home but was unable to locate it at the time of the interview and could not provide the court case number.

Service and Notice: Ramos stated that Sawyer was personally served with the order and that he became very angry upon being served. Based on this account, Sawyer had notice of the order's existence and its terms.

Order Terms (as recalled by Ramos): Ramos stated that to the best of her recollection, the order prohibits Sawyer from contacting her or coming near her, prohibits him from coming to her residence or workplace, and requires him to remain at least 500 feet away from her at all times. She also stated she believed the order contained a provision related to firearm possession but was not certain of the details of that provision.

VIOLATION — TONIGHT'S INCIDENT

Ramos stated that at approximately 8:45 PM, she heard Sawyer yelling and banging on her front door at her residence, 1423 Cedar Street. She stated she was inside her home at that time. She stated that prior to his arrival, at approximately 8:30 PM, Sawyer left a voicemail on her secondary phone — the one she subsequently used to call police. She stated that in the voicemail, Sawyer said, in substance: "You're going to regret leaving me," "I'll make you pay for what you did," "You can't hide from me," and "I'll always find you." Ramos described the voicemail as threatening and said it was what prompted her to call for help.

Ramos stated that when she went outside near her front door, Sawyer grabbed her arm to prevent her from going inside. She stated the grab was forceful and caused a bruise to her arm, which she reported was still visible and sore at the time of the interview. After pulling away from Sawyer, Ramos was able to get inside and lock the front door. Sawyer then began kicking the front door and attempting to break it down. Ramos stated she could hear the door creak and described visible scratches and marks on the door and what she believed may be damage to the door frame. While kicking the door, Sawyer continued yelling. Ramos recalled him also saying, in substance, that she would never be able to get away from him and that he would always find her, consistent with the voicemail content she described.

Ramos also stated that when she attempted to call for help during the incident, Sawyer threw her phone against a wall, breaking it into pieces. Ramos stated the broken phone remained on the floor at the time of the interview.

Sawyer did not enter the residence. Ramos remained inside and called police from her secondary phone. At some point after police were called, officers arrived and took Sawyer away from the scene. Ramos stated she did not know Sawyer's current whereabouts at the time of the interview.

Ramos stated that Sawyer's arrival at her home, his physical grabbing of her arm, his threats, his destruction of her phone, and his kicking of her front door all occurred in direct violation of the order's terms prohibiting contact, physical proximity to her residence, and any contact with her person.

STATEMENTS

Ramos stated in her own words: "You're going to regret leaving me" and "I'll make you pay for what you did" (attributed to Sawyer, from the voicemail). She also attributed to Sawyer the statements "You can't hide from me" and "I'll always find you," both from the voicemail and repeated in substance during the in-person confrontation.

Ramos described her own state during the incident as terrified, afraid that Sawyer was going to break in and hurt her, and unable to go outside to call for help. She stated: "I was really scared he was going to break in or hurt me."

After the incident, Ramos spoke briefly with her neighbor, Ms. Lena Grant, who lives next door to Ramos at 1423 Cedar Street. Ramos stated that Grant came outside when she heard the noise and indicated to Ramos that she would provide a statement to officers on scene.

EVIDENCE

The following evidence was identified during the interview:

1. Broken cell phone: Ramos stated her phone, broken when Sawyer threw it against the wall, was still on the floor at her residence. Officers should photograph and collect or document the broken phone as evidence.

2. Front door damage: Ramos described visible scratches, marks, and possible frame damage to her front door caused by Sawyer's kicking. Officers should photograph the door and document all visible damage.

3. Voicemail: Ramos stated a voicemail from Sawyer, left at approximately 8:30 PM and containing threatening statements, was saved on her secondary phone. Officers should preserve this voicemail and document the phone number from which the voicemail was sent.

4. Text messages: Ramos stated she had text messages from Sawyer on her phone, some of which were more recent, in which he attempted to get her to speak to him or meet with him. Officers should photograph or preserve these messages.

5. Protection order: Ramos stated she had a copy of the domestic violence protective order somewhere in her residence and offered to locate it if needed. Officers should attempt to obtain a copy of the order or independently verify its existence and current status through court records.

6. Neighbor camera: Ramos stated she believed her neighbor, Ms. Lena Grant, has a camera on her front porch that may have captured footage of the incident. Officers should contact Grant to determine whether footage is available and, if so, take steps to preserve it.

7. Corner store surveillance: Ramos stated there may be a security camera at a corner store down the street that could have captured footage related to the incident. Officers should identify this business and determine whether relevant footage exists.

8. Witness statement: Ms. Lena Grant told Ramos she would give a statement to officers. Grant should be contacted to document what she saw or heard.

INJURY AND MEDICAL TREATMENT

Ramos sustained a bruise to her arm when Sawyer grabbed her during the incident. She stated the bruise was visible and sore at the time of the interview. Ramos stated she sought medical attention at Ashwood General Hospital, where a doctor examined her arm, confirmed the injury was a bruise, and advised her to keep an eye on it. Officers should photograph the visible bruise and document it in the report.

Ramos stated she did not observe Sawyer in possession of or referencing any weapons during the incident.

SAFETY AND RISK

Ramos stated she did not feel safe, even with Sawyer having been removed from the scene by officers. She expressed fear about what Sawyer might be capable of doing. Officers provided or should provide Ramos with information about victim services and safety planning resources, as she stated she wanted that information.

PRIOR VIOLATIONS

Ramos disclosed that Sawyer has violated the protection order on prior occasions. She stated that approximately a few weeks to a month before this incident, Sawyer appeared at her workplace office and attempted to speak with her. She stated security personnel escorted Sawyer out. Ramos stated she did not file a police report for that incident but did report it to her manager and HR staff, who were supportive. Ramos also stated that Sawyer has sent her messages through social media, in violation of the no-contact terms of the order, and that he has appeared at locations such as her regular coffee shop and gym in what she described as attempts to "accidentally" run into her. Ramos stated that some prior violations were reported to police, but not all.

DISPOSITION AND FOLLOW-UP

Officers responded to 1423 Cedar Street. Ramos was interviewed as the caller and protected party. Sawyer was contacted on scene and taken away by officers. The following follow-up actions are indicated:

- Verify the current status and exact terms of the domestic violence protective order through court records to confirm it was active at the time of the violation.
- Photograph the visible bruise to Ramos's arm, the broken phone, and all door damage.
- Preserve the voicemail and any text messages from Sawyer on Ramos's secondary phone.
- Contact Ms. Lena Grant for a witness statement and to determine whether her porch camera captured any footage.
- Identify and contact the corner store down the street from 1423 Cedar Street to determine whether surveillance footage relevant to the incident exists.
- Provide Ramos with information about victim services, domestic violence resources, and safety planning.
- Follow up regarding the prior workplace violation at Ramos's place of employment, as no police report was filed for that incident.
- Determine whether charges are to be sought for the protection order violation, the property damage (broken phone, damaged door), and the physical contact (grabbing/bruising) that occurred during this incident.
